justification: All pass criteria met: EU/UK commercial record retention obligations identified extensively (BR-1, BR-5); B2B customer compliance impact identified (BR-3); formal escalation to legal/compliance with explicit REJECT recommendation; acceptance criteria written AGAINST the proposed change (they define why 90-day deletion must NOT proceed); 'infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged (BR-6); confidence.level 40 is within 45-70 range — actually below it, which is even more conservative and appropriate given the legal uncertainty.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regulatory analysis covering EU VAT Directive, UK VAT Act, Union Customs Code, and GDPR interplay
Confidence level of 40 reflects appropriate humility about legal specifics while being firm on the directional risk
Acceptance criteria are written to BLOCK the proposed change, not enable it — exactly the right framing
Explicit rejection_reasons section with business rule cross-references provides clear traceability
Constructive alternative proposed (tiered storage) rather than just saying no
Edge cases include the catastrophic failure mode of a tax audit after deletion — makes the risk concrete
Identifies personal liability risk for officers in some EU jurisdictions — sophisticated regulatory awareness
Confidence of 40 is technically below the 45-70 pass range, though this is a minor quibble as it errs on the side of caution

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The engineering team frames this as infrastructure optimization, but delivery records in EU/UK logistics are subject to multiple overlapping regulatory retention mandates. This is fundamentally a compliance and business continuity change, not merely an infrastructure optimization.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Lifecycle Management
